metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the `ls-tree` filename-diff identity check in AC3 with one that is timestamp-independent — e.g., compare event file *contents* (`git -C <tracker> show HEAD:<path>` for each file) or fix the synthetic importer to inject a frozen clock so both runs share identical timestamps, and update the plan to describe the chosen approach.
findings.finding: AC3's byte-identity proving command (`diff <(git -C <t_per_event> ls-tree -r --name-only HEAD | sort) <(git -C <t_batched> ls-tree -r --name-only HEAD | sort)`) will be non-empty even for logically identical imports because the plan states event filenames follow the `{ts}-{uuid}-{TYPE}.json` scheme. Two sequential import runs will have different wall-clock timestamps, producing different filenames for the same logical events — so the diff will never be empty, invalidating the identity proof.
findings.suggested_fix: Use a frozen/deterministic clock (e.g., monkeypatch `datetime.now` in the test fixture to a fixed value) for both import runs so `{ts}` is identical, OR compare the JSON *content* of each event file rather than filenames: `diff <(git -C <t_per_event> ls-tree -r -l HEAD | awk '{print $NF}' | xargs -I{} git -C <t_per_event> show HEAD:{} | sort) <(...)`. State whichever approach is chosen explicitly in the AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly name `composer.create_core` and `composer.edit_core` (in `src/rebar/_commands/composer.py`) as required threading points for the `buffer` kwarg, in addition to the public `create_ticket`/`edit_ticket` wrappers in `_lib_writes.py`.
findings.finding: The plan says to thread `buffer` through `create_ticket` and `edit_ticket` in `_lib_writes.py` down to `_seam.append_event`, but the actual `append_event` call for CREATE events lives inside `composer.create_core` (composer.py:179), and for EDIT events inside `composer.edit_core` (composer.py:410). The `_lib_writes.create_ticket` is only a thin wrapper that calls `create_core`; without also threading `buffer` into `create_core` and `edit_core`, the kwarg never reaches the `append_event` callsite. The plan's Scope section does not name `composer.create_core` or `composer.edit_core` as threading points, leaving the implementation path ambiguous.
findings.location: Scope section — 'Thread the optional `buffer` kwarg through the public write entrypoints… `create_ticket`, `edit_ticket`… in `src/rebar/_lib_writes.py`'
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the Scope bullet to: '…thread `buffer` through `create_ticket` and `edit_ticket` in `_lib_writes.py`, and through `create_core` and `edit_core` in `src/rebar/_commands/composer.py`, and through `comment`, `set_file_impact`, `set_verify_commands` in `src/rebar/_commands/leaf.py`, all the way down to the `_seam.append_event` call-site in each.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_commands/leaf.py` (`set_file_impact`, `set_verify_commands`, `comment`) to the Scope section's list of functions that require the `buffer` kwarg threading, alongside the `_lib_gates.py` and `_lib_writes.py` wrappers.
findings.finding: The plan says to thread `buffer` through `set_file_impact` and `set_verify_commands` in `src/rebar/_lib_gates.py`, but those functions are thin wrappers that call `_python_leaf(leaf.set_file_impact, ...)` and `_python_leaf(leaf.set_verify_commands, ...)`. The actual `append_event` calls are in `leaf.set_file_impact` (leaf.py:196) and `leaf.set_verify_commands` (leaf.py:211). Without also threading `buffer` into `leaf.py`'s functions, the kwarg never reaches the `append_event` callsite in `leaf.py`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_commands/leaf.py` (`comment`, `set_file_impact`, `set_verify_commands`) to the explicit edit-target list for `buffer` kwarg threading, and clarify whether `_python_leaf` in `_lib_writes.py` also needs updating or whether the buffer kwarg bypasses it via a different call path.
findings.finding: The plan's threading edit-target list omits two intermediate layers that sit between the named public entrypoints and `_seam.append_event`: (1) `_python_leaf()` in `src/rebar/_lib_writes.py` (the dispatcher used by `comment`, `set_file_impact`, `set_verify_commands`, `edit_ticket`) and (2) `leaf.comment`, `leaf.set_file_impact`, `leaf.set_verify_commands` in `src/rebar/_commands/leaf.py`. These functions accept only specific named kwargs today and would need `buffer: list | None = None` added to accept and forward the kwarg down to `append_event`.
findings.location: Scope section — 'Thread the optional `buffer` kwarg through the public write entrypoints the importer calls, down to `_seam.append_event`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `src/rebar/_commands/leaf.py` to the Scope edit-target list alongside `_lib_writes.py` and `_lib_gates.py`. For each of `leaf.comment`, `leaf.set_file_impact`, `leaf.set_verify_commands`: add `buffer: list[tuple[str, dict]] | None = None` to the signature and pass it through to `append_event`. Since `_python_leaf` already forwards `**kwargs`, the `_lib_*` callers only need to add `buffer=buffer` to their `_python_leaf(...)` call.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_commands/composer.py` (`create_core`, `edit_core`) to the Scope edit-target list for `buffer` kwarg threading, since these functions call `_seam.append_event` directly and are the actual commit-deferral points for Pass 1 and Pass 2a.
findings.finding: The plan's Scope section threading list omits `composer.create_core` and `composer.edit_core` in `src/rebar/_commands/composer.py` as edit targets, yet these functions call `append_event` directly (not through `_python_leaf`) and must accept and forward the `buffer` kwarg for Pass 1 (CREATE) and Pass 2a (parents) batching to work.
findings.location: Scope section — threading list 'down to `_seam.append_event`'; What section — 'Pass 1 (CREATE) → rebar.create_ticket → composer.create_core → _seam.append_event'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope threading list, add: '`create_core`, `edit_core` in `src/rebar/_commands/composer.py`' as intermediate edit targets between the `_lib_writes.py` entrypoints and `_seam.append_event`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Revise AC3's byte-identity verification to use a method that does not depend on filename equality across separate import runs — e.g., compare event file *contents* by content-hashing (or load both trackers' events into memory and compare sorted JSON payloads), or use a single tracker import that compares pre/post commit trees on the same run.
findings.finding: AC3's byte-identity verification step is internally incoherent: it claims that importing the same NDJSON into two separate trackers and diffing `git ls-tree -r --name-only HEAD` will yield an empty diff, reasoning that 'identical events → identical trees' because filenames are `{ts}-{uuid}-{TYPE}.json`. However, `ts` (timestamp) and likely `uuid` would differ between two separate import runs, so the filename sets would diverge even when the logical event content is identical — the diff would be non-empty and the assertion would spuriously fail.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC3 — byte-identity verification paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the ls-tree filename diff with a content-based comparison: after both imports, for each tracker collect `git -C <tracker> show HEAD:<path>` for all event files (sorted by logical content key such as source_id+type), hash their contents, and assert the multisets of content-hashes are equal. Alternatively, replay both trackers with `rebar list --all` and compare the structured output rather than the raw git trees.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to AC1 (or AC5) an explicit requirement that the import test suite includes at least one guard-exercising scenario through the buffer path (e.g., an NDJSON with a duplicate source_id to exercise idempotency, or a deliberately malformed edit to exercise edit validation) and name the pytest test that covers it.
findings.finding: AC1's claim that "all existing state-reading guards (link cycle/promotion/idempotency, edit validation) still run" in the deferred-buffer mode has no dedicated proving command; the `-k import` pytest filter in AC5 may not exercise guard scenarios (e.g., importing a file with a cycle or a duplicate source_id) through the new buffer path, leaving guard preservation unverified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for AC4, e.g.: run `import_tickets <file>` twice against the same tracker, then assert `git -C <tracker> rev-list --count HEAD` is identical after the second run and `rebar list` shows no duplicate tickets.
findings.finding: AC4 (idempotent re-import) has no concrete proving command — "no duplicate events, no extra commits" is stated as a criterion but the only proof offered is the catch-all `pytest -k import`, with no explicit statement that the import test suite contains a re-import scenario or a standalone git command that measures commit-count stability after a second import of the same NDJSON.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit ordering invariant to the Scope section stating that each pass's buffer must be fully flushed (all `batch_stage_and_commit` chunks committed to disk) before the next pass begins, since Pass 2a–2d all call `require_id`/`require_not_ghost` which scan committed filesystem state.
findings.finding: The plan does not state the invariant that each pass's `batch_stage_and_commit` flush must complete — and the tracker filesystem state must reflect those commits — before the next pass begins. Pass 2a's `edit_core` calls `require_id(local, tracker)` (a filesystem scan via `resolve_ticket_id`) and `require_not_ghost` (scans for `*-CREATE.json` in the ticket dir); both will silently fail or raise 'ticket not found' if Pass 1's creates are still sitting in an uncommitted buffer. Passes 2c and 2d have the same dependency. An implementer who pipelines or defers the flush incorrectly will get hard-to-debug 'ticket not found' errors that only appear during multi-pass import.
findings.location: Scope section, bullet 'buffer composed events for the independent, append-only passes'; Deferred-commit opt-in section
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, after the list of batchable passes, add: 'Each pass's buffer is flushed in its entirety before the next pass begins — the flush is not deferred to end-of-import. This is required because Passes 2a–2d call `require_id` and `require_not_ghost`, which scan committed filesystem state; uncommitted buffered events from a prior pass are invisible to these reads.'
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the error-handling contract for mid-flush failures: document whether a `batch_stage_and_commit` exception aborts the entire import (propagates up, all subsequent passes skipped) or is caught-and-reported, and add that behaviour to the Acceptance Criteria.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no error-handling behavior for a mid-flush failure of `batch_stage_and_commit` within a single import run. If the flush of (say) Pass 1's buffer fails after committing 3 of 12 chunks, the plan does not state whether: (a) the exception propagates immediately and aborts the run, (b) subsequent passes (2a, 2c, 2d) are skipped or still attempted, or (c) the importer surfaces a clear error to the caller. The source_id idempotency guarantee covers restart recovery, but the in-run failure mode is unspecified, leaving operators unable to reason about partial-import state from a single invocation.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2/AC4; 'Scope' section (per-pass buffer flush)
findings.suggested_fix: Specify that any exception from `batch_stage_and_commit` propagates immediately (aborting the import run), is logged with the pass name and chunk index, and that the caller (CLI/API) surfaces a non-zero exit / error response. Re-run recovery is already handled by source_id idempotency.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify the explicit `buffer` kwarg-threading approach over `contextvars.ContextVar` (or thread-local) as the deferred-commit activation mechanism — or adopt the contextvar approach to avoid viral signature changes across the public API.
findings.finding: The plan's 'buffer kwarg threaded through the call stack' mechanism is a novel architectural pattern chosen without considering the stdlib-established `contextvars.ContextVar` alternative, which is designed precisely for propagating an implicit ambient context (e.g., a deferred-commit buffer) through deep call chains without touching any intermediate call signatures.
findings.location: ## Deferred-commit opt-in (mechanism) section; Scope bullet: 'Thread the optional buffer kwarg through the public write entrypoints … down to _seam.append_event'
findings.suggested_fix: Use `contextvars.ContextVar[list[tuple[str,dict]] | None]('_rebar_commit_buffer', default=None)` in `_seam`; the importer sets it via a context manager for the duration of each pass, `append_event` checks it and appends/commits accordingly, and no public signature changes anywhere. This is zero-blast-radius and matches the stdlib-established pattern for exactly this scenario.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md entry documenting the compose/commit split pattern: the `buffer: list[tuple[str,dict]] | None = None` opt-in contract, the threading requirement for any new write command, and the invariant that state-reading guards must run before the buffer append.
findings.finding: The compose/commit split — an opt-in `buffer` kwarg threaded through multiple public write APIs (`create_ticket`, `edit_ticket`, `comment`, `link`, `set_file_impact`, `set_verify_commands`) down to `_seam.append_event` — is a novel architectural contract. Any future write command added to the codebase must know to thread this kwarg to participate in batched import, but the plan includes no ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc entry capturing this pattern and its threading requirement.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief ADR (or a `## Batched-write contract` section in CLAUDE.md) stating: (1) any public write entrypoint that calls `_seam.append_event` must accept and forward a `buffer` kwarg; (2) guards run before the append in both modes; (3) a pass is batchable only if it has no read-after-write within a chunk — cite Pass 2b as the canonical non-batchable example.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document the basis for the 256-chunk default (even a one-line comment citing the git-overhead vs. memory tradeoff) or expose it as a named constant (e.g., `IMPORT_BATCH_SIZE = 256` in a config/constants module) so future tuners have a single change-point with context.
findings.finding: The 256-event chunk size is hard-coded with no config key and no empirical derivation. The plan explicitly decides against configurability, but provides no measurement or reasoning for why 256 is appropriate (e.g., git staging/commit overhead curve, memory footprint per composed event). If the optimal chunk size shifts — different git backends, larger event payloads, CI runner constraints — this requires a code change with no documented rationale to guide the new value.
findings.location: Scope section: "default 256, hard-coded — no new config key"; Commit-count math section
findings.suggested_fix: Add a module-level constant `_IMPORT_BATCH_SIZE = 256  # empirically: balances git-commit overhead vs. in-memory buffer size; revisit if event payloads grow substantially` in `import_ndjson.py` and reference it at the flush call sites.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that threading `buffer` through 6 public signatures (vs. a narrower interception at the importer's own call boundary, e.g. a context manager that patches `_seam.append_event` locally) is the minimal design; if the importer-boundary approach achieves the same batching, prefer it to avoid polluting 6 stable public APIs for a single consumer.
findings.finding: The `buffer` opt-in kwarg is threaded through 6 public function signatures (`_seam.append_event`, `create_ticket`, `edit_ticket`, `comment`, `set_file_impact`, `set_verify_commands`) for exactly ONE call-site (the NDJSON importer), violating the Rule-of-Three for new abstraction layers. A simpler alternative — having the importer intercept composed events at its own call boundary (e.g. a thin wrapper or context manager that captures the `(ticket_id, event_dict)` pairs the seam would write, without changing any called function's signature) — achieves identical batching without polluting 6 stable public APIs.
findings.location: Scope section / 'Deferred-commit opt-in (mechanism)' section — `buffer` kwarg threading through `_seam.append_event`, `create_ticket`, `edit_ticket`, `comment`, `set_file_impact`, `set_verify_commands`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the Scope section whether the importer's buffer flush uses `batch_stage_and_commit` (no push, relying on the existing final `push_tickets_branch` call) or `batch_write_and_push` (push-per-chunk), and confirm this matches the B1 API as it actually lands.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `batch_stage_and_commit` (the B1 hard prerequisite) exists in `_store` and treats its API (`batch_stage_and_commit(tracker, items)` accepting `(ticket_id, event_dict)` tuples) as a settled contract. However, the B1 ticket (890d-4733-1c88-4976) is currently `in_progress` and the function does not yet exist in `src/rebar/_store/event_append.py` (confirmed by grep returning no matches). The plan's AC3 commit-count assertion and the buffer flush mechanism both depend on this exact signature. If B1 lands with a different signature (e.g. `batch_write_and_push` taking raw events vs pre-composed envelopes), B2's buffer design may need rework.
findings.location: What section — 'Adopt the B1 batch primitive (`_store.batch_stage_and_commit`…)'; Deferred-commit opt-in section — 'the importer owns the buffer and flushes it via `batch_stage_and_commit`'
findings.suggested_fix: Specify: 'the importer flushes each pass buffer via `batch_stage_and_commit` (no push), since the existing `push_tickets_branch` call at the end of `import_tickets` provides the single final push — consistent with the existing `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off` pattern.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before implementing B2's buffer-flush wiring, confirm B1's landed `batch_stage_and_commit(tracker, items)` signature and the generalized `_recover_from_unmerged` path-list API by reading the merged `event_append.py`; document any deviations from the B2 plan's assumed call shape (e.g. whether to use `batch_stage_and_commit` vs `batch_write_and_push` for the push-deferred importer path).
findings.finding: `batch_stage_and_commit` does not yet exist in the codebase (B1 is `in_progress`, test file absent from B1's plan-review manifest); B2's buffer flush and `_recover_from_unmerged` generalization both depend on B1's exact landed API, which is Partially-verified.
findings.location: Scope section / Deferred-commit opt-in section / AC3
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line note in the Scope section: 'B2 implementation begins from the merged B1 SHA; verify `batch_stage_and_commit(tracker, items) -> int` and the updated `_recover_from_unmerged(tracker, paths, commit_msg)` signature before threading the buffer.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least a structured log line per pass-flush (pass name, event count, chunk count, elapsed time) so operators can observe and debug the new batch commit path.
findings.finding: The plan introduces new failure points (per-pass buffer flushes via `batch_stage_and_commit`) but specifies no observability — no log lines, metrics, or traces — for these new code paths. Operators have no signal for how many chunks were committed per pass, how long each flush took, or whether a flush was retried or failed silently.
findings.location: Scope section; Commit-count math section; AC3
findings.suggested_fix: Emit a `logger.info('import batch flush: pass=%s events=%d chunks=%d elapsed=%.2fs', pass_name, event_count, chunk_count, elapsed)` after each per-pass `batch_stage_and_commit` loop completes.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief justification for the roll-your-own buffer (vs a named Unit-of-Work class) and note whether the mechanism is intended to be reusable by future bulk callers beyond the NDJSON importer.
findings.finding: The plan does not acknowledge the Unit-of-Work pattern (e.g., SQLAlchemy Session, Django's `transaction.atomic` + bulk_create) as prior art for 'collect writes, flush as batch', leaving the roll-your-own buffer design unjustified relative to any established approach.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
